transcript.text: Hi and welcome to the book lab. I'm
Bjorn and today we're taking a look at
the best and most interesting
non-fiction releases for April 2026. I
have picked six books to highlight
covering purpose and meaning, the
neuroscience of attachment, American
philosophy, the science of belief, and
maybe one of the most important uh
questions you can ask yourself, what to
do with the life. Let's jump right into
it.
Um, the first book on the list is What
Makes a Life. It's by Jim Collins. You
probably know his name from his hit
book, uh, Good to Great. A book that I
didn't really connect with, but this new
one sounds very interesting. It's out on
April 7th. So, Good to Great was one of
the most successful business books. And
this book is not about business. This is
Jim Collins asking what to do with a
life. He spent a decade studying this.
He follows uh remarkable people through
what he calls the cliff. those moment
when life gets fract fractured and you
have to reinvent yourself. Two rock
musicians uh losing their band. Two
public figures rebuilding after a
scandal. Two suffergists who achieved
their goal and then face the question
what now? He studies these turning
points side by side looking for what
separates the people who rebuild from
those who don't. So, I love that this is
more about personal questions than just
business. And for the first time, we
also get Jim Collins's uh own life
story. This book resonated with me
because I'm at a turning point myself. I
at work, we're working on a project that
we're going to launch in April, very
very soon, in a couple of weeks. And
that's like the do or die moment for us.
And I might have to look for another
career if that doesn't fall through.
Everything depends on this project.
Otherwise, I would just have to find a
new job and that's not easy because of
the layout layoffs in the games
industry. So maybe I need to reinvent
myself. That's why this book piqu my
interest. Now next book, Secure the
revolutionary guide to creating a secure
life by Amir Levine. And you might know
that author because it's uh from a book
that I really like. This book here we go
attached. You might have heard of this
one. It's a very popular book on
attachment theory. Um there are three of
them according to these authors. It's
the securely attached, the anxiously
attached and avoidant um personality
trait. And this basically shapes how you
build relationships and if you can make
them last. This is a long- aaited
follow-up. It's been 15 years since this
book came out. Dr. Amir Levvin is back
with a bigger promise. Anyone can learn
how to create a secure life. And it's
not just about relationships this time.
Lavine takes the attachment framework
and expands it to everything. Your
health, your career, uh how you handle
social media, even how you deal with job
job searching. People who operate in a
secure mode, as he calls it, are
healthier, more resilient, less
susceptible to consumerism, and better
at navigating the digitiz world. The
bold claim here is that security isn't
something you are born with. It is
something that you can learn. I read
attached several years years ago and it
fundamentally changed how I look at
relationships. And if the science in
this book is uh good h which I think it
is because it was in the other book then
this might be one of the most important
psychology books that you read this
year. If you like books uh like the ones
by Yuan Hari um Robert Sapolski Gabber
for that matter then this might be a
book for you. Uh next book is the
Emerson Cycle by Bruce Nicole's. It's
out on April 28th. This is one for the
philosophy and history fans. In 1840
Concord, Massachusetts, a small town
became home to some of the most
revolutionary thinkers in American
history. Ralph Waldo Emerson, uh, Henry
David Thorough, Nathaniel Haltor, Louisa
May Alcott, they formed what became
known as the Emerson Circle, debating
democracy, equality, individualism, and
what it means to think for yourself. I
think this book has a very compelling
format. Uh, it would bring us into the
life of these philosophers, hear about
their struggles, their breakthroughs,
their aha moments. Uh, I've been getting
deeper and deeper into philosophy. And
this just sounds like a really solid
book, a summer read maybe, or maybe a
spring read for that matter. Not a dry
and heavy text book, but something that
just can spark your imagination and you
can learn a thing or two about the
thoughts of these people. I have read
books about the existentialists, but the
transcendentalists,
they are some people that I need to
explore a little bit more. Even though I
read uh things like Walden and Civil
Disobedience, uh I would like to know
more and this is a book that I would
pick up. The next book is uh The Meaning
of Your Life: Finding Purpose in an Age
of Emptiness. It's by author C. Brook
and it's out on March 31st. I actually
have two books on this list that just
missed last list and it's like the last
day of March, so it's basically April,
right? Author C. Brooks, he had a book
called Strength to Strength about
finding success and meaning in the
second half of your life. It was one of
those books that had you rethink how you
spend your life. This one goes even
deeper. Brooks argues that millions of
people feel a growing sense of
emptiness, a lack of purpose and
significance. And there is a scientific
reason for it. Rapid cultural, economic,
and technological changes are literally
rewiring our brains. Reducing our
ability to perceive depth and meaning.
So, what is the meaning of life? Uh,
well, Brooke suggests that it all starts
with a journey. I get a little bit of a
man's search for meaning by Victor
Frankle vibe from this one. I think this
is a great book to pick up if you're
into the more deeper questions this uh
this April. It's also a strong pairing
with Colin's book that is about those
crossroads where we really have to
choose our path and choose wisely. Next
up is another leard. It came out on
March I think it was or will be actually
recording this before March 20 31st and
it's the infinity machine by Sebastian
Malabi Disab
and the quest for super intelligence.
Uh, as you know, we are heading into the
singularity. I have to cover these books
because I'm interested in this will also
impact everyone's life regardless of
what you think about the AI craze.
Things are just going to change so fast.
We really need to stay focused and
understand what's going on. A revelatory
portrait of the visionary behind Google
Deep Mind and the race to control the
future and what it means to win. Even in
a tech world crowded with visionary
leaders, Demis Hasabis is recognized as
a special case. Born in the working
class immigrant parents in North London,
a chess prodigy at by five, a wizard
coder in his teens, he turned down a
sevenf figureure job offer from a video
game studio to study science in
Cambridge. Not long after this, he
started the groundbreaking company Deep
Mind uh AI project. He was obsessed with
AI. He had a belief that AI would solve
the world's hardest problems, change
life and work as we know it, and maybe
even unlock the answers to the secrets
of the universe. I don't know much about
this guy myself. It's actually the first
time I hear about his name. But this
book has me uh really interested. I love
the or origin stories of these big
companies that now are everywhere in the
news and in the media and they are
actually changing the world as the
vision uh foretold. The result is a
revelationpacked portrait of a singular
mind and a historic reckoning with the
AI revolution. So if you want to pick up
one AI book this month and I think and I
think actually this is it. It feels like
it's part biography but also you get the
history of Deep Mind and this is an
important company. Those were the books
for this month. I hope you enjoyed the
list. Uh it was quite um sparce I think.
I think May will be uh more jam-packed
actually, but there are some good ones
and I'm going to create uh or I'm going
to pick a pick of the month as I always
do. And I think I will actually choose
secure. I really love Attached. I have a
review of of it. Um that I can link to
in the description below or you can just
search for Attached to Book Lab. Uh this
was a book that opened up my eyes to
some things that I didn't really know
about before that I couldn't see. And
now I see it everywhere and I can really
use the tools from this book to navigate
relationships. Even if it's like
romantic relationships, your relatives,
your annoying mother, mother and
father-in-law, it's really helpful. So,
I have big hopes for Secure. It's my
pick of the month. Let me know in the
comments which one you would pick and
read first. And if you didn't find
anything in this list that really
interested you, then check out my 2026
reading list. It contains a lot of niche
books, a lot of interesting, deep books.
Or just check uh out the other monthly
breakdowns. There might be books that
you have missed. I know I have a website
out well where you have all the lists.
This is lists after list after list. You
will find something uh that you like to
read on there. And also I'm launching a
newsletter that I'm experience
experimenting with. So if you want to be
one of the first people on that, then
sign up. It's going to be really short
and full of just book recommendations
and hidden gems and all of that. Anyway,
happy reading and see you next week with
more book reviews. Uh I'm going to
review Foreign Height 451. Another
classic in the bag. I'm reading The
Fabric of Reality. I'm bringing this
one, The Curious Mind of Elon Musk. That
was pick of the month last month.
Actually, it's a really good book. Uh I
got it from the publisher. Uh so it was
actually the sponsored book for that
month, but I started to look into it and
now I've read half of it. So I got
hooked. But I always want to mention
mention that it's a collaboration with
the author. Um happy reading. Burn out.
